summary: This advice column addresses various reader questions ranging from family dynamics to academic career decisions. The centerpiece is advice about dealing with an assertive older sister, where Aunt Pythia draws on self-reflection to suggest the sister likely wants engagement rather than submission. Other topics include the unverified ABC conjecture proof, Obamacare enrollment data as an engineering-becomes-political issue, and advice for someone considering leaving industry for a PhD. The column maintains a conversational, self-deprecating tone throughout.
key_points: Assertive people often want to be challenged rather than deferred to - their strong opinions are invitations for debate, not final pronouncements
Engineering failures are increasingly becoming political issues (Knight Capital, Obamacare) as technology systems become more central to society
You never get rid of problems, you just get a new set of problems - career decisions are about which problems suit your personality
Politics exists everywhere including academia and startups; the key is finding work absorbing enough that you can tune it out
Family obligations aren't absolute - it's acceptable to distance yourself from family members who make you feel suffocated
money_quotes: The funny thing about family is, we are our most vulnerable with our family, and yet we are also very comfortable with them, because we know them so well.
She's a know-it-all loudmouth, true, but the sooner you can either get on her wavelength or roll your eyes and love her in spite of her pushy know-it-all ways the better for you and for her.
I don't agree that you have to hang out with family, unless possibly if they're dying or in need.
We are increasingly seeing pure engineering issues become political issues.
The nerds are taking over, in other words, or at least their mistakes are.
My motto is 'You never get rid of your problems, you just get a new set of problems.'
If you're really allergic to politics, I suggest you find a place where you can safely ignore that stuff, like maybe in a cave in the woods.
key_insight: Strong personalities often want engagement and challenge rather than deference, and what feels like dominance may actually be an invitation to participate.
